topic: reflecting on the year — grateful for family and health

turns.content: I am in a strange mood tonight. Not bad — reflective. I was sitting on the porch after dinner and it hit me that this past year has been one of the most meaningful of my life. I just want to talk about that for a moment, if you do not mind.
I don't mind at all. Reflective moods deserve space. What is it about this past year that's standing out to you?

Family. That is the answer to everything, really. I have spent so many years focused on the bench — on other people's families — that sometimes I forget to stop and look at my own. But this year I have been more present. More attentive. And I can feel the difference.

That awareness is significant. Being present isn't passive — it requires you to actively step out of the professional role and into the personal one, which for someone in a demanding career is harder than it sounds. What does being more present look like for you? Is it specific moments, or more of a general shift?

Both. It is in the small things. Sitting with Carlos in the evening without looking at case files. Actually tasting the food at dinner instead of eating while I think about tomorrow's docket. Calling my sister just to talk, not because there is something to coordinate. I used to think those things were luxuries. Now I think they are the whole point.

There's a shift that happens when you stop treating presence as a luxury and start treating it as the foundation. It sounds like you've crossed that line. The fact that you can taste dinner, that you can sit with your husband without the mental pull of work — those aren't small things. They're evidence that you've restructured your priorities, even if you didn't do it consciously.

Does Carlos notice the difference?

He does. He does not make a big speech about it — that is not his way. But he smiles more. He suggests things — a walk, a movie, let's drive to the beach — and I think he is testing whether I will say yes. And lately I have been saying yes. That is new for me. For a long time, the answer was always 'not tonight, I have work.'

Saying yes is such a simple thing, and such a powerful one. When someone you love has been hearing 'not tonight' for years and you start saying yes, you're not just agreeing to a walk or a movie — you're telling them they matter more than the work. Carlos testing the waters and finding them warm is probably one of the best things that's happened in your marriage recently, even if neither of you would say it that dramatically.

We would not say it that dramatically, no. But you are right. It is exactly that. And my health — Dios mio, I do not take that for granted anymore. At my age, you start seeing people your age have problems. Serious problems. And I look at myself and I am healthy, I am strong, I can do everything I want to do. That is not something I earned — it is something I was given, and I am grateful for it every day.

Gratitude for health becomes sharper when you start seeing it taken from people around you. It's not morbid — it's clarity. And that clarity often feeds back into the other changes you're describing. When you recognize that your time and your body are not guaranteed, suddenly sitting on the porch after dinner becomes an event, not a waste of time.

The combination of being present with family and appreciating your health — those two things reinforce each other. Being healthy lets you be present. Being present makes you want to protect your health. It's a good cycle to be in.

It is a good cycle. And I think about my parents — everything they sacrificed so that I could sit on this porch, in this house, with this life. They did not get to enjoy a season like this. They were always working, always surviving. I have the privilege of reflecting because they did the hard part first.

That's a profound way to frame it. Your parents built the foundation under pressure so that you could build a life on top of it — and part of honoring that sacrifice is actually enjoying what they made possible. If they were always surviving, then your ability to sit on the porch and feel grateful is the payoff of their labor. That's not indulgence. That's the success they were working toward, even if they never got to experience it themselves.

I think they'd be proud of what you're describing tonight — not the career, not the title, but the fact that their daughter can stop and feel full.

You are going to make me cry, and I do not cry easily. But yes. I think they would be proud. My father would say something practical like 'good, now go to bed, it's late.' And my mother would just hold my face and not say anything. That was her way.

That image — your mother holding your face without words — says more than any speech could. Some people show love through language and some through presence. It sounds like your mother was pure presence, and you've inherited that. The way you describe sitting with Carlos, tasting dinner, saying yes to a walk — that's her in you.

I never thought of it that way, but you are right. She did not need words. She just needed to be in the room, and you felt it. Ay, I carry her with me every day. In the kitchen, in the courtroom, on that porch. She is in all of it. And Carlos — that man sits beside me on the porch and says nothing, and somehow it is everything. We are two quiet people sitting in the dark and it is the best part of my day.

Two quiet people sitting in the dark, and it's the best part of your day. That might be the most honest description of a good marriage I've ever heard. No performance, no agenda — just presence. That's rare, and the fact that you recognize it as precious says you're paying attention to the right things.

Your mother would recognize that porch moment immediately. She'd understand it without explanation.
She would. All right. I am going to take my father's imaginary advice and go to bed. Thank you for listening to an old woman get sentimental on a Tuesday night.

You are not old, and there is nothing wrong with being sentimental on a Tuesday. What you described tonight — the presence, the gratitude, the awareness of what your parents gave you — that's wisdom, not sentimentality. And it sounds like you've earned a very good season of life.

Go get some rest. Carlos is probably already asleep, and the porch will be there tomorrow evening. Buenas noches.
